text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic lower interior for a distal sigmoid cancer in a woman.
The surgeon suspends the large uterus at the beginning of the procedure to improve access to the surgical field.
The surgeon places a sponge to retract the small bowel away from the medial aspect of the sigmoid mesentery, then initiates a medial to lateral dissection.
I left this case largely unedited for the transvaginal extraction because that's the part that I really wanted to focus on with this video.
So a lot of lateral dissection was left out. The anastomosis was left out just because I think you've seen that all before.
And again, the purpose of this video is to highlight the ease of doing a transvaginal extraction for a cancer.
This is a higher BMI patient, probably about a only about five feet tall and a lot of visceral fat.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) for high ligation, using scissors to meticulously clear the vascular adventitia and expose the vessel wall.
The surgeon uses scissors to complete the circumferential dissection around the isolated inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and then switches to a vessel sealer to perform the high ligation.
The surgeon mobilizes a portion of the upper rectum, highlighting the hypogastric plexus nerves adjacent to the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the hypogastric plexus nerves, which are visible in the surgical field.
For patients with smaller tumors or less bulky mesentery, I would probably try to do a transrectal extraction. With this specimen, that just was not going to be possible.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, joining the previously dissected medial plane, in preparation for transvaginal extraction.

A sponge is placed to indicate the previously dissected area and ensure it is positioned above the ureter. The surgeon uses scissors to perform the majority of the lateral dissection. As the dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure, the surgeon switches to a vessel sealer, which also functions as a grasper.
The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure, moving over the left kidney, using a technique that appears faster and more hemostatic.
The surgeon completes lateral mobilization and high ligation without fully mobilizing the splenic flexure, due to the patient's redundant sigmoid and descending colon.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection reaching up to the splenic flexure, highlighting the completion of this part of the procedure.
The surgeon addresses congenital adhesions of the omentum attached to the left anterior abdominal wall, carefully dissecting them down while continuing the dissection around the left kidney.
The surgeon identifies the spleen in the distance and begins dissecting a small splenial area at the top of the left kidney.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer for hemostasis near the splenial, which is visible but distinct from the spleen proper.
The surgeon mobilizes the left side of the rectum, having previously completed dissection on the right side. The upper rectum is carefully mobilized as part of the procedure.
The surgeon proceeds to take the distal mesentery, likely using surgical instruments to separate and manage the mesenteric tissue.
I have started to put my twelve millimeter trocar as arm number three through the right rectus rather than in the right lower quadrant. I do this for two reasons.

I think that you actually get a really nice angle for stapling a little bit deeper in the pelvis. But additionally, the closure of the port through the right rectus, I just close the anterior fascia as opposed to doing a full thickness suture passer through the right lower quadrant. And I think that there's less pain associated with that.

So for my benign and malignant disease, when I use a stapler, I have been putting the stapler there. And I adopted that because basically that's what I do whenever I do a low anterior with a diverting ileostomy. I'll put my stapler in that location. And I've now
The surgeon transitions to the resection phase, dividing the proximal section with visible pumping blood flow, followed by the division of the distal section using a stapler at a favorable angle.
The surgeon uses a 60mm stapler to perform a resection, elevates the uterus, and places an EEA sizer into the posterior vagina.
The surgeon positions themselves and carefully maneuvers the EEA sizer near the anterior wall of the rectum, ensuring precise placement without being too close to the rectal wall.
The surgeon opens the posterior fornix of the vagina, located high in the pouch of Douglas, while seated at the console.

The surgeon introduces a 15mm Endocatch bag into the surgical field to extract the specimen. The bag is opened outside the patient, and the end staple line is grasped with a ring clamp to facilitate the removal of the bulky specimen.
The surgeon addresses bleeding from the corner of the vagina by closing the area with a 3-0 V-Loc suture, which helps control the bleeding.
The surgeon begins closing the muscular wall of the vagina, noting that the vaginotomy is larger than the original incision due to tearing when the larger specimen was pulled through.
The surgeon uses arms number one and three to suture, with the camera positioned in arm number two located in the epigastric midline, making it easy to see the edges and facilitate closure.

The surgeon continues to clean the surgical area, preparing to close the peritoneum. The muscular wall of the vagina is already closed. The left ureter is visible just beyond the surgeon's left hand at the ten to eleven o'clock position.

You can be surprisingly close to this at this part of the procedure, so just be cognizant of that. I did not have ureteral stents in this case, as I often do not for benign disease without previously perforated diverticulitis or for just a sigmoid cancer.

The surgeon runs a few throws of suture and cuts, visually shortening the rectum. A rigid scope is used to check for any stitching or tearing from the transvaginal extraction, confirming none occurred. The sewn anastomosis is aligned, with two posterior rows of staple ends sewn together using a 2-0 PDS suture. The inner layer is sewn with a 3-0 V-Lock suture, extending to the left and right.

The surgeon applies the second outer layer of IIOPDS sutures, imbricating the inner IIIOV lock while taking substantial bites of the bowel wall. The bowel is shown coming together without tension. The surgeon finishes by suturing towards the original posterior IIOPDS, held up by the left hand, and ties the sutures together. The ureter is briefly shown again.

The surgeon moves the camera to arm number three to focus on the anastomosis site. A 19mm proctoscope is inserted through the anastomosis to perform a leak test, confirming the site is hemostatic, widely patent, and without any leakage.

The surgeon cleans the surgical area, revealing the proximity of the rectal anastomosis and the vaginal suture line. The omentum is sutured to the pouch of Douglas, with a brief accidental suture pull-through. The procedure is performed in a Trendelenburg position at 26 degrees.

The surgeon uses arm number one to hold the omentum in place, interposing it between the rectal and vaginal suture lines. A second stitch is placed for reinforcement. The uterus is then dropped down, and bipolar cautery is applied to the suture line to ensure hemostasis.
Minimal pain with these cases, very quick recovery. I think it's an excellent option to add to your repertoire. I hope you enjoyed.
